title: Generative AI at work: Survey evidence from three Central Banks

study_design: In the spring of 2024, we partnered with three central banks to run surveys on generative AI in the workplace: the National Bank of Slovakia, the Czech National Bank, and the European Central Bank.
The survey was designed to understand key questions regarding generative AI use within these samples.
We focused on large language models since these are the generative AI tool that is most applicable to the work done in central banking.
The survey module consisted of five modules.
This survey data from all three samples complements one another to provide a comprehensive look at how generative AI is used in central banking.

On the one hand, the sample from the National Bank of Slovakia was collected alongside a forthcoming field experiment on generative AI in the workplace (Maršál and Perkowski, 2024), and covers employees throughout the entire organizational hierarchy.
On the other hand, our sample from the Czech National Bank and the European Central Bank focuses on a specific subgroup: PhD economists in central banks.
These surveys were conducted as a part of a seminar on how economists can use generative AI, and thus permits a deeper look at how this specialized group uses generative AI at work.
task: We asked participants about their use of generative AI across various workplace tasks.

Both samples required the same 10 general tasks where generative AI can be helpful: (i) writing emails or reports, (ii) generating code or debugging, (iii) creating content (articles, blogs, etc), (iv) data analysis and visualization, (v) educational purposes or learning new skills, (vi) customer service, (vii) research and information gathering; (ix) design and creative work, (x) other.
In addition, the ECB sample included workplace tasks specifically related to the work done by PhD economists.

These tasks included: (i) conducting literature reviews, (ii) cleaning data and preprocessing, (ii) exploratory data analysis, (iv) statistical modeling and econometric analysis, (v) generating economic models, simulations, or forecasts, (vi) writing research papers or reports, (vii) drafting policy recommendations, (viii) explaining economic concepts or theories, (ix) answering research questions, (x) peer review or providing feedback, (xi) research ideation and brainstorming, (xii) coding or programming tasks; (xiii) creating presentations or teaching materials; (xiv) project or task management.

For each of the general tasks, participants reported whether they have used generative AI for the task, which generative AI tool(s) they have used for the task, and how many hours in an average week they used generative AI tools for the task.
population: We partnered with three central banks – the European Central Bank (ECB), the National Bank of Slovakia (NBS), and the Czech National Bank (CNB) – to collect novel survey data on generative AI use at work and employee beliefs regarding the technology.
Table 1 displays summary statistics for the entire sample of participants across both pools.
The sample is highly well-educated: over 90 percent of participants have a masters degree or higher.
37 percent of the sample is female, and 15 percent are managers.
The average respondent is 41 years old and has spent 9 years at their organization.
On average participants work for 45 hours a week.
Almost a third of their work time is spent on analytical tasks, a quarter on non-routine cognitive tasks, and just under 20 percent on routine cognitive tasks.
The remaining quarter of their time is spent primarily on leadership and management tasks, and interpersonal and communication tasks.
N=345 (Full Sample), N=162 (NBS sample), N=183 (Economist sample)
outcome_definitions: We asked participants several questions regarding their exposure to and knowledge of generative AI.
Participants reported whether they had heard of generative AI tools before, which tools they have heard of, and whether they have used generative AI before.
We asked participants about their use of generative AI across various workplace tasks.

For each of the general tasks, participants reported whether they have used generative AI for the task, which generative AI tool(s) they have used for the task, and how many hours in an average week they used generative AI tools for the task.
We asked participants a series of questions to gauge their attitudes toward generative AI.

These questions spanned several subthemes, including (i) fears of automation, (ii) reputational considerations at work, (iii) trust in generative AI, (iv) workplace redesign with generative AI, (v) technical knowledge of generative AI, and (vi) barriers to workplace adoption.
These questions were collected using a 7-point Likert scale from strongly disagree to strongly agree.
We asked participants to predict the percentage of employees who have previously used generative AI and who have used it in the workplace.
We also asked participants to predict the impact of generative AI use on their own productivity.
statistical_results: Almost 90 percent of the sample has heard of large language models before, and 76 percent of participants have used a large language model before.
However, only 45 percent of the sample has used a large language model in the workplace, versus over 60 percent for personal use.

Those who use generative AI at work are on average younger (37.8 years old vs 43 years old), are more likely to be male (30 percent of generative AI users are female, while between 40 and 45 percent of non-users are female), and spend more of their time at work using the English language (36 percent vs between 20 and 32 percent for non-work users).
Over half of workplace users reported using generative AI for coding or debugging, writing email and reports, and educational purposes or learning new skills.
Around 44 percent used it for translation.
Another third reported using generative AI for research and information gathering, creating content, and data analysis.
The median respondent reported using generative AI for three workplace tasks.
Only 13 percent of those who use generative AI in the workplace only use it for one task, while around 40 percent use generative AI for four or more job tasks.
The median respondent reported using generative AI for only one economics task.
Meanwhile, over 40 percent of respondents reported using it for no economics tasks.

87 percent of users who use generative AI tools for emails report using ChatGPT, 91 percent of those who use generative AI for coding, 92 percent for those who use generative AI for learning new skills, and 91 percent for those who use generative AI for research and information gathering.
Participants are unlikely to use multiple tools at once: the averages in column 1 are all close to one.
The median user reports spending 9 percent of their work week interacting with generative AI at work.
Assuming a 40 hour work week, this is between 3 and 4 hours a week.
However, there are some "super-generative AI" users, who report spending more than 20 percent of their work week alongside this technology.
These individuals are rare, though: most users report less than 5 percent of their work week interacting with generative AI.

Only 11 percent of respondents believe that advances in generative AI will lead to massive unemployment (versus 80 percent who disagree), and just 12 percent worried their job is at risk of automation (versus 79 percent who disagree).
Instead, respondents believe generative AI will complement labor (77 percent agree versus 14 percent disagree).

While respondents are skeptical that generative AI will reduce their autonomy at work (9 percent agree versus 80 percent disagree), there are some concerns regarding workplace monitoring: almost a quarter of responses believe that generative AI will increase workplace monitoring.

Non-PhD economists are twice as likely than PhD economists to believe that advances in generative AI will lead to massive unemployment (15 percent versus 7 percent), and almost twice as likely to believe that generative AI will increase workplace monitoring (28 percent versus 15 percent).

If anything, PhD economists are more likely to believe their skills will be less valuable with generative AI (20 percent versus 14 percent) and worry their job is at risk of automation (12 percent versus 9 percent).
On the one hand, respondents themselves do not judge their coworkers less favorably if their coworker used a large language model for their work (84 percent versus 5 percent).
However, a larger percentage of respondents believe that their coworkers and managers would have negative evaluations of their work if use of generative AI was revealed.
For example, 20 percent of respondents believe their co-workers would negatively judge their work if they used generative AI.
Interestingly, the magnitude is much smaller for manager evaluations, where only 8 percent would be uncomfortable with their manager knowing they used generative AI for their work.
Moreover, almost 30 percent of generative AI workplace users use the technology to ask questions they are shy to ask about.

PhD economists are 25 percentage points less likely to be comfortable with their manager knowing they used a large language model for their work (61 percent versus 86 percent), and more than twice as likely to not want their coworker to know about their generative AI usage at work (15 versus 7 percent).
59 percent disagree that they have no idea how large language models work (versus 22 percent who agree).
80 percent understand that large language models are statistical models of prediction, and over half understand that these models do not understand the meaning or context behind their output.

One area of misinformed beliefs is regarding the knowledge base of these tools: over 80 percent of participants wrongly believe that current large language models dynamically update their knowledge bases.
On average, employees believe that they would be almost 14 percent more productive if they used a large language model at work.

Men believed that the productivity benefits of generative AI were 26 percent (= (14.0 − 11.1)/11.1) higher than woman, and generative AI workplace users believed the productivity benefits were around 60 (= (16.6 − 10.3)/10.3) percent higher than non-workplace users.

Overall, the sample was split on whether effectively using generative AI would require structural changes at the bank (43 percent agree versus 34 percent disagree), with limited differences across samples.
There was more agreement on executive and managerial support for generative AI, with over 50 percent agreeing versus less than 10 percent disagreeing.
This also included interesting differences, whereby employees are 9 percentage points more likely to agree that team managers support generative AI usage versus bank executives.
Participants were split on their knowledge and awareness of the organization's rules regarding generative AI at work, with an average score of 0.1 and 0.2.
Instead, 37 percent were uncertain about the simplicity and ease of following the bank's rules on generative AI.
Lastly, employees gave the most support for successful use cases in increasing generative AI use at work, followed by clear support from bank executives.
We find that workplace task composition accounts for just 12 percent of the variation in generative AI usage at work.
Generative AI users report an average productivity impact of 16.6 percent versus 10.3 percent for non-workplace users.
ambiguity_or_caveats: Given the addition of the survey questions for the economic tasks for the ECB sample, we had to drop some survey questions for this sample.

We have data on whether each participant in the ECB sample used generative AI across the various economic tasks plus how many hours a week they spent using generative AI on these tasks, but not which tools were used.
This was done to prevent participant fatigue during the survey.
One reason why central banks hesitate to fully integrate generative AI is concern over data privacy.
Central banks handle sensitive data, and there is a legitimate fear that using AI models like ChatGPT, which rely on vast external datasets, could inadvertently expose confidential information.
Our results indicate that the privacy concerns documented above are not the primary obstacles preventing widespread adoption of generative AI in central banking.
Instead, almost 95% of central bankers identify the lack of clear use cases as the main cause.
Employees remain uncertain about how to effectively integrate these tools into their workflows.

Indeed, results from our survey of generative AI users finds that usage is much lower for specialized workplace tasks versus more generalist ones, highlighting the need for demonstration of use cases for more specialized central banking tasks.

We also find that while over 50% of central bank employees agree that bank leadership supports the usage of GAI, 88% of them identifies insufficient bank support as reason for not extending their usage of gen AI at work.
Thus, employees believe a lack of clear use cases and guidance from bank executives curtails generative AI integration at work.
These results suggest that there may be barriers in fully embracing these tools for tasks that require a higher level of domain expertise or specialized knowledge.
This suggests there may be productivity benefits from workers targeting the right specialized tools to the right tasks.
Further research on how workers trade-off the benefits versus costs of various tools, and how to incentive worker exploration across these AI tools, will be helpful in this regard.
This indicates that across users, there is substantial heterogeneity in the extent to which the technology is integrated into their workday.
Meanwhile, Panel B indicates that the most time is spent interacting with generative AI for coding, for learning new schools, for conducting research, and for translation.
Overall, these results indicate that even among those who use generative AI at work, generative AI usage does not make up a lot of their work time.
This suggests that there may be further opportunities to increase usage, perhaps through the display of use cases.
The results in Table 4 provide some evidence of pluralistic ignorance in the reputational consequences of generative AI.
Thus, it appears that individuals believe there will be negative evaluations from co-workers, not managers.
No consistent pattern emerges in the trust questions.

Although participants are more likely to trust large language models (53 percent) versus not trust them (28 percent), they seem skeptical about the output of large language models and their development.
Meanwhile, participants seem decently informed about large language models.
Conditional on the same job type, participants who have larger beliefs in the productivity impact of generative AI are much more likely to use generative AI at work.
This lines up with the results in Table 5, which shows that generative AI users report an average productivity impact of 16.6 percent versus 10.3 percent for non-workplace users.

The results in Figure 4b control for the task make-up of one's job and suggest that the presentation of successful use cases may be especially useful in boosting generative AI adoption in the workplace.
The results in Figure 4b also highlight the role that reputational considerations play in adoption decisions.
Those who report higher reputational fears associated with generative AI usage are less likely to use generative AI at work.
This suggests that fostering a culture of acceptance regarding generative AI usage may be key to increasing adoption.
Interestingly, we see no evidence of a relationship between trust in generative AI and usage at work.

Overall, generative AI workplace adoption seems to be driven by the task make-up of work, individuals' beliefs regarding the productivity impact of generative AI, and reputational concerns regarding generative AI usage at work.

notes: This is a survey-based observational study with no experimental manipulation or control group. The paper examines generative AI adoption and usage patterns across three central banks (National Bank of Slovakia, Czech National Bank, and European Central Bank) through employee surveys conducted in spring 2024. The study includes both a broad organizational sample (NBS) and a specialized sample of PhD economists (ECB and CNB). The paper does not compare AI vs. no-AI conditions but rather examines correlates and predictors of voluntary adoption among workers. IRB approval was obtained (WCG IRB protocol 20241892).
